title: Pancreatic beta-cell proliferation methods and device
abstract: The invention provides a biocompatible device for invasive phototherapy comprising a light emitting phototherapy source (LEPTS) connected with an outlet suitable for emitting light to a target, wherein the outlet is suitable to be located implanted in a patient suffering from diabetes into an area within or in close proximity to the pancreas, to deliver adequate light intensity onto the pancreatic beta-cells and/or the pancreas, and the LEPTS is suitable to irradiate the target pancreatic beta-cells with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light to activate pancreatic opsin proteins; and a method of ex vivo treatment of a population of human pancreatic beta-cells to promote cell growth, proliferation and/or insulin production, comprising a) providing a preparation of human pancreatic beta-cells; b) treating the cells by exposure to light with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light thereby activating pancreatic opsin proteins in the treated cells.
claims:
Claims 1 . A biocompatible device for invasive phototherapy comprising a light emitting phototherapy source (LEPTS) incorporating an outlet or connected with an outlet, suitable for emitting light to a target, wherein the outlet and optionally the LEPTS or the device is suitable to be located implanted in a patient suffering from diabetes into an area within or in close proximity to the pancreas, to deliver adequate light intensity onto the pancreatic beta-cells and/or the pancreas, and the LEPTS is suitable to irradiate the target pancreatic beta-cells with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light to activate pancreatic opsin proteins. 2. The device of claim 1 , wherein the LEPTS is suitable for irradiating the pancreas with visible light at one or more wavelengths ranging from 400 to 650 nm. 3. The device of claim 1 or 2, wherein the LEPTS is suitable for irradiating the pancreas with an effective energy dose ranging from 1 iiW/cm 2 to 10 mW/cm 2 . 4. The device of any of claims 1 to 3, which comprises signal communication means for repeatedly transmitting light to the target pancreatic beta-cells with the same or a different energy dose according to a predefined dose regimen. 5. The device of any of claims 1 to 4, wherein the LEPTS is suitable to be positioned outside the patient's body. 6. The device of any of claims 1 to 4, wherein the LEPTS is suitable to be implanted into the patient's body. 7. The device of any of claims 1 to 4, which is an implantable device. 8. A pharmaceutical composition comprising an active substance for treating diabetes in a patient, for use in combination with a device of any of claims 1 to 7, preferably wherein the active substance is an antidiabetic agent or retinal. 9. A method of ex vivo treatment of a population of human pancreatic beta-cells to promote cell growth, proliferation and/or insulin production, comprising a) providing a preparation of human pancreatic beta-cells; b) treating the cells by exposure to light with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light thereby activating pancreatic opsin proteins in the treated cells; and optionally further c) determining the extent of cell growth, proliferation and/or insulin production in the treated cells. 10. The method of claim 9, wherein the wavelength is ranging from 400 to 650 nm. 1 1 . The method of claim 9 or 10, wherein the effective energy dose is ranging from 1 LiW/cm 2 to 10 mW/cm 2 . 12. The method of any of claims 9 to 1 1 , wherein the opsin proteins are one or more of a) human short-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 1 ; b) human medium-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 2; c) human long-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 3; d) human rhodopsin characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 4; e) human opsin-3 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 5; f) human melanopsin characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 6; or g) human opsin-5 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 7. 13. A preparation of human pancreatic beta-cells with preserved and/or protected beta -cell function, obtainable by a method of any of claims 9 to 12. 14. The preparation of claim 13, for use in a method of treating a patient suffering from diabetes, preferably wherein the pancreatic beta -cells are autologous to said patient. 15. Use of a LEPTS for ex vivo treatment of isolated pancreatic beta-cells to control cell growth, proliferation and/or insulin production, by exposing the cells to light with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light to activate pancreatic opsin proteins. 16. A method of treating a patient suffering from diabetes by invasive phototherapy, comprising activating target beta-cells in the pancreas of said patient by irradiation using a device comprising a LEPTS incorporating an outlet or connected with an outlet, which is suitable for emitting light to the target beta-cells, comprising the steps: a) inserting the outlet and optionally the LEPTS into an area within or in close proximity to the pancreas, thereby delivering adequate light intensity onto the target beta-cells and/or the pancreas, and 

 b) irradiating the target beta-cells with an effective energy dose, using at least one wavelength of visible light thereby activating pancreatic opsin proteins. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein said wavelength of visible light is ranging from 400 to 650 nm. 18. The method of claim 16 or 17, wherein said effective energy dose is ranging from 1 uW/cm 2 to 10 mW/cm 2 . 19. The method of any of claims 16 to 18, wherein the opsin proteins are one or more of a) human short-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 1 ; b) human medium-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 2; c) human long-wave-sensitive opsin 1 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 3; d) human rhodopsin characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 4; e) human opsin-3 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 5; f) human melanopsin characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 6; or g) human opsin-5 characterized by the amino acid sequence SEQ ID 7. 20. The method of any of claims 16 to 19, wherein the target beta -cells are repeatedly irradiated according to step b). 21 . The method of any of claims 16 to 20, wherein the outlet is positioned within the patient's body and is connected to the LEPTS that is positioned outside the patient's body. 22. The method of any of claims 16 to 20, wherein the outlet is part of the LEPTS which is implanted into the patient's body. 23. The method of any of claims 16 to 22, which is combined with a treatment by administering an active substance for treating diabetes. 24. A method of treating a patient suffering from diabetes by administering an effective amount of a preparation of human pancreatic beta -cells according to claim 13. 25. The method of claim 24, wherein the pancreatic beta-cells are autologous to said patient.
